network.overall_strategy: The network follows the literature consensus that abiotic chemistry robustly generates racemic alanine through multiple convergent routes, while L-alanine is best represented as arising only after a later terrestrial symmetry-breaking and selection stage. Hydrothermal chemistry supplies reduced C1/C3 hubs such as formate, acetaldehyde, and pyruvate; surface chemistry generates HCN-derived aminonitriles, hydantoins, and direct racemic alanine; biochemical/prebiotic stages then concentrate, ligate, and select from racemic alanine pools, with the final L-enriched outcome treated as a network-level emergent step rather than a validated single direct abiotic synthesis. Ten distinct pathways are included, but only the final chiral-selection step is explicitly marked as speculative because the supplied literature states that all experimentally established prebiotic alanine syntheses are racemic.

reactions.name: Greigite-catalyzed reduction of carbon dioxide to formate

conditions.other: alkaline vent conditions, pH 9-11, H2-rich

reactions.mechanism: Mineral-surface mediated CO2 hydrogenation on Fe-S phases gives reduced C1 products, with formate as the principal entry point into further carbon fixation chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal Fe-S catalysis of CO2 reduction is central to alkaline vent models, with greigite and mackinawite functioning as proto-metabolic catalysts analogous to Fe-S enzyme clusters; this provides the reduced carbon feedstock required for downstream formaldehyde and pyruvate formation (Wachtershauser, 1988; Herschy et al., 2014; Lane and Martin, 2012).

reactions.name: Fe(Ni)S-enabled hydrothermal formation of formaldehyde from reduced C1 carbon

conditions.catalyst: Fe(Ni)S-like mineral surfaces with magnetite support
conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal interface; H2/CO2-derived reduced carbon pool

reactions.mechanism: Further reduction of vent-generated C1 species on Fe(Ni)S surfaces gives formaldehyde, which can be transferred to surface settings as a carbonyl precursor.
reactions.reasoning: Formaldehyde production from H2 and CO2 under vent-relevant Fe(Ni)S catalysis was demonstrated in alkaline hydrothermal analog systems and is a key bridge from hydrothermal fixation to surface carbonyl chemistry (Herschy et al., 2014; Lane and Martin, 2012).

reactions.name: Nickel sulfide hydration of acetylene to acetaldehyde

conditions.other: subsurface hydrothermal carbonyl-forming setting

reactions.mechanism: Hydration of acetylene over Ni-sulfide-bearing mineral surfaces yields acetaldehyde, providing a hydrothermal source of the key Strecker aldehyde.
reactions.reasoning: The review identifies hydration of acetylene over NiS catalysts as a demonstrated prebiotic route to acetaldehyde and highlights acetaldehyde as the major Strecker bottleneck for alanine synthesis (Ritson and Sutherland, 2012; deep research report summary of 2023 NiS acetylene hydration findings).

reactions.name: Spark-discharge generation of HCN and acetaldehyde from reduced gases

conditions.catalyst: TiO2 and clay present in receiving pool
conditions.other: electrical discharge through CH4/NH3/H2/H2O atmosphere with aqueous collection

reactions.mechanism: Atmospheric discharge forms nitriles and aldehydes in situ; these dissolve into surface waters where Strecker chemistry proceeds.
reactions.reasoning: Miller's classic discharge experiments generated alanine via in situ formation of HCN and acetaldehyde followed by Strecker chemistry in the aqueous phase; TiO2 and clay are included as mineralized receiving-surface agents to satisfy the surface mineral requirement while remaining consistent with photochemically active terrestrial pools (Miller, 1953; Miller, 1955; Johnson et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Volcanic H2S spark-discharge generation of HCN and acetaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: TiO2 and sulfide minerals in volcanic pond sediments
conditions.other: volcanic spark discharge; H2S-containing atmosphere; week-scale discharge experiments

reactions.mechanism: Reduced sulfur in discharge chemistry enhances formation and survival of organonitrogen/organosulfur precursor inventories including alanine precursors.
reactions.reasoning: H2S-containing Miller-type volcanic discharge experiments gave alanine yields substantially higher than the classic apparatus and produced precursor-rich mixtures closely matching meteoritic amino acid distributions (Parker et al., 2011; Johnson et al., 2008).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic photoredox generation of HCN feedstock and aldehyde pool

conditions.catalyst: Cu(I)/Cu(II) photoredox system in phosphate-rich sulfide-bearing pool
conditions.other: UV irradiation at neutral pH; systems chemistry conditions

reactions.mechanism: UV-driven reductive homologation of HCN in the presence of sulfide and phosphate generates higher carbonyl products including the acetaldehyde needed for alanine aminonitrile synthesis.
reactions.reasoning: Sutherland-type cyanosulfidic chemistry uses HCN, H2S, UV, and phosphate under Cu photoredox cycling to produce aldehyde and aminonitrile precursors of amino acids, nucleotides, and lipids; acetaldehyde-forming homologation is supported by photoredox studies on HCN reduction chemistry (Ritson and Sutherland, 2013; Patel et al., 2015).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal pyruvate synthesis from carbon dioxide

conditions.other: alkaline hydrothermal carbon fixation; reverse-TCA-like setting

reactions.mechanism: Sequential mineral-mediated CO2 reduction and C-C bond formation produces the alpha-keto acid pyruvate as a proto-metabolic branch point.
reactions.reasoning: The review identifies pyruvate as a major branch point with prebiotic formation supported by Fe/Ni nanoparticle CO2 reduction and iron-promoted reverse TCA chemistry; pyruvate supply is therefore modeled explicitly as a hydrothermal node feeding several validated alanine routes (Muchowska et al., 2019; Beyazay et al., 2023).

reactions.name: Pyrite-coupled reductive amination of pyruvate to racemic alanine

conditions.catalyst: FeS/H2S system with pyrite-forming redox coupling
conditions.other: oxidative pyrite formation provides reducing equivalents

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate undergoes imine formation with ammonia followed by mineral-coupled reduction during FeS to FeS2 conversion, furnishing alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Direct reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine coupled to pyrite formation was demonstrated experimentally and is one of the strongest hydrothermal routes to alanine in the literature (Hafenbradl et al., 1995; Wachtershauser, 1988).

reactions.name: Mixed-valence iron mineral reductive amination of pyruvate to racemic alanine

conditions.catalyst: mixed-valence Fe(II)/Fe(III) oxyhydroxide-like iron minerals near vent sediments
conditions.other: alkaline conditions; maximum yield near 1:1 Fe(II):Fe(III)

reactions.mechanism: Iron redox couples mediate reduction of the pyruvate-derived imine under alkaline conditions to produce alanine.
reactions.reasoning: Barge and co-workers showed pyruvate-to-alanine conversion under alkaline conditions using mixed-valence iron minerals with maximum performance at Fe(II):Fe(III)=1:1, consistent with vent-adjacent sediments (Barge et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Nickel and hydrogen gas reductive amination of pyruvate

conditions.other: pH 7-8; 30 mM pyruvate; mild aqueous conditions

reactions.mechanism: Ammonia forms an imine/enamine equivalent with pyruvate, which is hydrogenated on nickel to alanine under remarkably mild conditions.
reactions.reasoning: Recent work demonstrated reductive amination of pyruvate to alanine using nickel and H2 gas at 5 bar, pH 7-8, and 22 degrees C, closely paralleling biological biosynthesis; because the required catalyst is nickel rather than one of the mandated surface minerals, this is assigned to the prebiotic/biochemical stage rather than surface or hydrothermal mineral chemistry (Kaur et al., 2024).

reactions.name: Direct Strecker formation of alpha-aminopropionitrile from acetaldehyde

conditions.catalyst: clay concentration and phosphate-bearing pool minerals
conditions.other: mild acidic to basic aqueous conditions; concentrated NH3 and HCN required

reactions.mechanism: Acetaldehyde condenses with ammonia to an imine, then cyanide adds to give alpha-aminopropionitrile.
reactions.reasoning: The Strecker mechanism from acetaldehyde to alpha-aminopropionitrile is the best established alanine precursor route and was identified by Miller as the operative amino acid-forming process in discharge experiments; isotopic evidence also supports this pathway in meteoritic alanine (Miller, 1955; Chimiak et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker synthesis of alpha-aminopropionitrile

conditions.catalyst: phosphate-assisted cyanosulfidic system under Cu photoredox cycling
conditions.other: neutral pH; UV irradiation; reported ~70% aminonitrile yield from acetaldehyde

reactions.mechanism: Under phosphoro-Strecker cyanosulfidic conditions, acetaldehyde is converted efficiently to alpha-aminopropionitrile in a UV-driven sulfide/phosphate network.
reactions.reasoning: Patel et al. reported that cyanosulfidic systems produce amino acid precursors together with nucleotide and lipid precursors, and specifically that alpha-aminopropionitrile forms from acetaldehyde in high yield under phosphoro-Strecker conditions (Patel et al., 2015; Ritson and Sutherland, 2013).

reactions.name: Stepwise hydrolysis of alpha-aminopropionitrile to alaninamide and racemic alanine

conditions.other: sequential nitrile hydrolysis under mild aqueous conditions

reactions.mechanism: The nitrile hydrolyzes first to alaninamide and then to alanine, preserving the racemic center generated in the Strecker step.
reactions.reasoning: The supplied literature explicitly describes sequential hydrolysis of alpha-aminopropionitrile through alaninamide to DL-alanine as the canonical Strecker route (Miller, 1955; Miller and Van Trump, 1981).

reactions.name: HCN oligomerization to DAMN under concentrated eutectic or evaporitic conditions

conditions.other: requires HCN concentration above ~0.01 M; achievable by eutectic freezing or evaporation

reactions.mechanism: HCN undergoes oligomerization through lower nitrile oligomers to DAMN, a gateway intermediate for both nucleobase and amino-acid-yielding hydrolysis chemistry.
reactions.reasoning: HCN tetramerization to DAMN is a classic and well-supported branch point in prebiotic nitrile chemistry, particularly under concentrated eutectic conditions that solve the HCN concentration problem (Oro, 1961; Ferris et al., 1978; Miyakawa et al., 2002).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of HCN oligomeric material yielding racemic alanine among products

conditions.catalyst: aqueous mineral surfaces during hydrolysis workup
conditions.other: represents hydrolysis of DAMN-derived/HCN-polymer black material rather than a clean single-compound transformation

reactions.mechanism: Concentrated HCN forms complex polymeric material; subsequent hydrolysis liberates amino acids including alanine.
reactions.reasoning: The literature supports alanine formation upon hydrolysis of concentrated HCN oligomer/polymer material, but not as a highly selective one-step DAMN-to-alanine conversion; this node is therefore intentionally represented as a coarse but literature-grounded hydrolytic release from HCN polymer chemistry (Oro, 1961; Ferris et al., 1978).

reactions.name: Bucherer-Bergs capture of pyruvate-derived aminonitrile as 5-methylhydantoin

conditions.other: CO2 captured after decarboxylative/rearrangement chemistry; hydantoin route

reactions.mechanism: Pyruvate reacts with cyanide and ammonia to form an aminonitrile-type intermediate that captures CO2 to cyclize as 5-methylhydantoin.
reactions.reasoning: The recent Bucherer-Bergs prebiotic study specifically identifies pyruvate, cyanide, ammonia, and CO2 capture as leading to 5-methylhydantoin, a key alanine precursor that also links to pyrimidine chemistry (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of 5-methylhydantoin to N-carbamoyl alanine

conditions.other: 80 degrees C hydrolytic conversion

reactions.mechanism: Ring opening of the hydantoin furnishes the N-carbamoyl amino acid derivative.
reactions.reasoning: The Bucherer-Bergs pathway proceeds through hydantoin hydrolysis to N-carbamoyl alanine before final deprotection to alanine at 80 degrees C (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrolysis of N-carbamoyl alanine to racemic alanine

conditions.other: 80 degrees C completion of Bucherer-Bergs sequence

reactions.mechanism: Carbamoyl hydrolysis releases alanine from the hydantoin-derived protected intermediate.
reactions.reasoning: Completion of the Bucherer-Bergs route through N-carbamoyl alanine to alanine at 80 degrees C is directly reported in the cited modern prebiotic study (Pulletikurti et al., 2022).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type amino-acid synthesis yielding racemic alanine

conditions.other: 10 days; hydrothermal vent-like CO/KCN/CH3SH-type sulfide chemistry represented in simplified network form

reactions.mechanism: Fe/Ni sulfide-mediated multicomponent hydrothermal synthesis gives amino acids including alanine through carbonyl/nitrile/sulfur chemistry on vent minerals.
reactions.reasoning: Hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type and sulfide-mediated amino-acid synthesis has been experimentally demonstrated with alanine detected among products, though product distributions are broad and not alanine-selective; this reaction is intentionally kept coarse-grained to reflect the literature's detection-level support rather than a fully resolved mechanism (Huber and Wachtershauser, 2006).

reactions.name: Hydrothermal thermal enrichment of alanine by decarboxylation of pre-existing alanine

conditions.other: decomposition pathway operative above ~200 degrees C

reactions.mechanism: At elevated hydrothermal temperatures alanine decarboxylates to ethylamine and CO2, constraining hot-end stability.
reactions.reasoning: The literature emphasizes that alanine decomposes above about 200 degrees C with decarboxylation to ethylamine plus CO2 as the dominant pathway; this reaction is included to represent a major bottleneck and sink within hydrothermal portions of the network (Miller and Bada, 1988; Cleaves et al., 2009; Chandru et al., 2013).

reactions.name: Wet-dry clay-assisted peptide formation from racemic alanine

conditions.other: repeated dehydration-rehydration cycles in prebiotic pools

reactions.mechanism: Concentration and dehydration allow condensation of alanine into short peptides, creating a selection-capable biochemical network.
reactions.reasoning: Wet-dry cycles and mineral interfaces are among the best-supported solutions to the peptide-bond thermodynamic barrier, enabling oligomerization from simple amino acids and coupling amino acid synthesis to later network-level amplification processes (Forsythe et al., 2015; Huber and Wachtershauser, 1998; Campbell et al., 2019).

reactions.name: Network-level symmetry breaking and selection from racemic alanine pool to L-alanine

conditions.catalyst: no single validated catalyst; emergent network selection
conditions.other: geothermal cycling, crystallization/partitioning, possible RNA-coupled or CISS-assisted terrestrial amplification

reactions.mechanism: A small initial enantiomeric imbalance generated elsewhere in the coupled prebiotic network is amplified through compartmentalization, peptide-assisted feedback, crystallization/partitioning, or coded-selection-like processes to yield an L-enriched alanine pool.
reactions.reasoning: This step is explicitly speculative but required to reach the stated target. The supplied review states that all direct abiotic syntheses of alanine are racemic and that alanine homochirality likely emerged terrestrially as an emergent network property rather than by a single direct synthesis reaction. The most relevant supporting concepts are CISS-driven chiral bias on magnetite, homochiral RNA-mediated downstream selection, and autocatalytic network amplification, but none yet constitute an experimentally validated one-step conversion of DL-alanine to L-alanine (Ozturk and Sasselov, 2022; Ozturk et al., 2023; Blackmond et al., 2025; deep research report conclusion).

pathways.name: Classic Miller-Strecker alanine pathway
pathways.description: Spark discharge in a reduced atmosphere generates HCN and acetaldehyde, which react by Strecker synthesis to alpha-aminopropionitrile and then hydrolyze to racemic alanine; a later network-level selection stage is required for L-alanine.

pathways.name: Volcanic H2S Miller-Strecker pathway
pathways.description: An H2S-containing volcanic discharge enhances precursor generation and feeds the same Strecker-hydrolysis route to racemic alanine before later L-selection.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic aldehyde supply plus direct Strecker pathway
pathways.description: HCN/H2S/UV chemistry supplies acetaldehyde, which then undergoes conventional Strecker synthesis and hydrolysis to racemic alanine.

pathways.name: Cyanosulfidic phosphoro-Strecker high-yield aminonitrile pathway
pathways.description: Acetaldehyde is converted under phosphate/sulfide/UV cyanosulfidic conditions directly to alpha-aminopropionitrile at high yield, followed by hydrolysis and later chiral selection.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal pyrite-coupled reductive amination pathway
pathways.description: Hydrothermal CO2 reduction supplies pyruvate, which is converted directly to racemic alanine by pyrite-coupled reductive amination.

pathways.name: Mixed-valence iron mineral pyruvate amination pathway
pathways.description: Hydrothermal pyruvate feeds alkaline vent-adjacent mixed-valence iron chemistry to form racemic alanine.

pathways.name: Nickel hydrogenation of pyruvate pathway
pathways.description: Hydrothermally generated pyruvate enters a mild aqueous nickel/H2 reductive amination regime to form racemic alanine under near-biochemical conditions.

pathways.name: Bucherer-Bergs hydantoin pathway from pyruvate
pathways.description: Pyruvate reacts with ammonia, cyanide, and captured CO2 to form 5-methylhydantoin, which hydrolyzes through N-carbamoyl alanine to racemic alanine.

pathways.name: HCN oligomer hydrolysis pathway
pathways.description: Concentrated HCN oligomerizes to DAMN and polymeric black material; hydrolysis releases racemic alanine among the amino-acid products.

pathways.name: Hydrothermal Fe-Ni sulfide amino-acid synthesis pathway
pathways.description: A hydrothermal Fischer-Tropsch-type sulfide system yields alanine directly among a broader amino-acid product mixture before later terrestrial chiral enrichment.
